caveats: a: No six-digit wage-by-task study was found.
a: Vendor-described capability is not measured labor displacement.
a: The supplied compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to an IPS basis.
rho: The only window-and-door-specific implementation evidence found is vendor-authored.
rho: Census AI adoption data cover all nonfarm businesses, not this industry.
rho: Custom products and frequent changeovers reduce the reliability of fixed automation assumptions.
e: NAICS does not separate custom service-like fabrication from standard product manufacture.
e: Repeat product orders do not automatically qualify as an outsourced service.
e: The supplied LMM firm count is a margin-bridged estimate based on 2022 receipts and a January 2026 sector margin.
s5: Intent is not completion.
s5: Gallup covers all industries and small-business sizes.
s5: Its transfer category includes gifts and rare IPOs that may not qualify.
q: No direct five-year AI-benefit retention study exists for this industry.
q: Owens Corning is much larger than the target firms.
q: Capture differs materially between custom architectural products and standardized production-builder units.
d5: Current housing data are a point in a volatile cycle.
d5: LIRA is nominal, one-year, and not specific to windows and doors.
d5: Public-company door commentary also includes non-wood products and markets outside the US.
o: Insourcing and product substitution can overlap with demand change rather than operator replacement.
o: Custom architectural windows require more accountability than standardized components.
o: No direct customer survey measures future self-service in this category.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Eligible firms receive repeat B2B orders and earn revenue through customer-specific fabrication and production services bundled into the price of physical window and door units; standard catalog manufacturing remains product revenue and is outside the lens.
lens.excluded_activities: Standard commodity product sales without an outsourced-service relationship, on-site finish carpentry, installers that do not manufacture, captive plants, distributors, software vendors, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market wood window and door manufacturers that repeatedly provide outsourced custom fabrication, configuration, prehanging, finishing, or specification-controlled production to builders, dealers, distributors, or other external customers.

a.bridge: BLS occupation counts describe the broader other-wood-product manufacturing workforce, and O*NET describes generic production-helper tasks rather than wage-weighted NAICS 321911 tasks. These are mapped to the mix of cutting, assembly, glazing, finishing, inspection, quoting, configuration, and order handling in window and door plants. The broader mix may understate digital configuration exposure but overstate transferability where physical handling dominates.
caveats: No six-digit wage-by-task study was found.
Vendor-described capability is not measured labor displacement.
The supplied compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to an IPS basis.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task census from representative 321911 plants, separating labor hours for cutting, assembly, finishing, glazing, inspection, engineering, configuration, estimating, scheduling, order entry, and warranty handling, with current automation baselines.
a.rationale: The broader wood-products workforce is concentrated in assemblers, woodworking-machine operators, material movers, saw operators, truck operators, and production supervisors. Physical fabrication and handling are difficult for AI alone, but visual inspection, order configuration, estimating, scheduling, design checks, maintenance prediction, documentation, and customer service are exposed. A window-and-door vision vendor demonstrates a concrete inspection workflow covering scratches, seals, glazing, and hardware, supporting meaningful but not majority task exposure.

d5.bridge: Housing starts, broad remodeling spending, and a large door producer's market commentary are proxies for constant-price, constant-quality demand for the eligible custom-manufacturing service basket. Nominal remodeling spending can overstate real quantity because LIRA is not inflation-adjusted, while new-construction weakness may understate replacement demand for aging homes.
caveats: Current housing data are a point in a volatile cycle.
LIRA is nominal, one-year, and not specific to windows and doors.
Public-company door commentary also includes non-wood products and markets outside the US.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year unit forecasts by the Window & Door Manufacturers Association or Freedonia segmented by wood products, new construction versus replacement, custom versus standard units, and US region, reconciled to Census starts and inflation-adjusted remodeling data.
d5.rationale: Demand is driven by residential new construction and repair/remodeling. Census reported May 2026 housing starts 8.7% below the prior year, while Harvard projected remodeling-spending growth to slow sharply in early 2027. Owens Corning also describes near-term North American construction and discretionary remodeling as challenged. Replacement needs and eventual housing normalization support a near-flat-to-modest-growth five-year base, but cyclical downside remains substantial.

caveats: NAICS does not separate custom service-like fabrication from standard product manufacture.
Repeat product orders do not automatically qualify as an outsourced service.
The supplied LMM firm count is a margin-bridged estimate based on 2022 receipts and a January 2026 sector margin.
e.change_evidence: Firm-by-firm classification of the supplied 234-firm LMM estimate using contracts and revenue by custom fabrication, prehanging, finishing, project configuration, repeat builder programs, standard catalog products, installation, and distribution.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is manufacturing, not services, but customer-specific configuration, prehanging, finishing, and made-to-order production can function as repeat outsourced fabrication for builders and dealers. Owens Corning identifies customization and service as competitive methods in doors, suggesting a meaningful subset, while standard catalog manufacturers remain outside the fixed lens.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 321911 ($3,848,108,251) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 321911 ($19,129,790,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $308,495 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 321911); class <5: 277 firms, avg revenue/firm $612,264 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $89,697 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 196 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,346,837 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $197,312 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 168 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,659,196 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $389,572 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 183 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,826,678 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,293,108 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 51 firms, avg revenue/firm $33,010,020 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,835,968 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 45 firms, avg revenue/firm $332,237,222 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $48,672,753 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 234

caveats: Insourcing and product substitution can overlap with demand change rather than operator replacement.
Custom architectural windows require more accountability than standardized components.
No direct customer survey measures future self-service in this category.
o.change_evidence: Builder, dealer, architect, and distributor interviews on five-year plans for direct sourcing, imports, insourcing, material substitution, modular production, and acceptance of fully automated suppliers without local engineering and warranty accountability.
o.rationale: Windows and doors remain physical, code- and specification-sensitive building components. Software can configure, sell, and inspect them but cannot eliminate accountable fabrication, quality control, warranty support, and delivery. Some demand may shift to vertically integrated builders, imports, standardized products, or non-wood substitutes, leaving the estimate below one.

caveats: No direct five-year AI-benefit retention study exists for this industry.
Owens Corning is much larger than the target firms.
Capture differs materially between custom architectural products and standardized production-builder units.
q.change_evidence: Eligible-firm contract and bid analysis covering price-reset cadence, rebates, cost indexation, open-book clauses, builder program renewals, customer concentration, warranty credits, and gross-margin changes following measured automation projects.
q.rationale: Customer-specific products and service, quality, customization, lead time, and warranty performance provide more differentiation than commodity panels, allowing some productivity benefit to remain embedded in gross margin. Yet builders and dealers solicit alternatives and compete on price; at renewals they can demand sharing of visible labor savings. Benefits from lower defects, faster quoting, and working-capital reduction should be less transparent and more retainable than direct unit-labor reductions.

caveats: The only window-and-door-specific implementation evidence found is vendor-authored.
Census AI adoption data cover all nonfarm businesses, not this industry.
Custom products and frequent changeovers reduce the reliability of fixed automation assumptions.
rho.change_evidence: Plant deployment cohorts reporting implementation time, capital cost, inspection accuracy by SKU, false-reject rates, labor hours per unit, rework, warranty claims, quote cycle time, and sustained headcount avoidance over at least 12 months.
rho.rationale: Quoting, configuration, scheduling, document processing, and warranty triage can be implemented with modest integration, while in-line inspection and closed-loop process changes require cameras, stable master data, controls work, and human exception handling. High SKU variation is both an AI use case and a deployment challenge. Five years should permit multiple workflows, but safety, quality, fragmented systems, and limited technical staff constrain full realization.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year stated sale, gift, IPO, or ownership-transfer plans across US small-business owners rather than completed qualifying control transfers among LMM wood window and door manufacturers. The 14% all-owner figure is nudged upward for the likely mature ownership of fabrication firms, but completion failure and nonqualifying transfers pull in the opposite direction.
caveats: Intent is not completion.
Gallup covers all industries and small-business sizes.
Its transfer category includes gifts and rare IPOs that may not qualify.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age and succession interviews for eligible firms plus five years of closed control transactions from PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, broker records, and state filings, excluding internal reorganizations and non-control capital events.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports 14% of working owners planning a sale or ownership transfer within five years and 17% among owners age 55 or older. A 15% base is reasonable for a fragmented industrial population with many long-established private firms, subject to a wide range because actual closed control transfers may be lower than plans.

principal_driver: AI-enabled configuration, inspection, scheduling, and warranty reduction in repeat custom-production relationships.
principal_weakness: The NAICS code is fundamentally product manufacturing, so only a minority of firms plausibly fit the recurring outsourced-service lens.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as manufacturing wood or wood-clad windows, doors, frames, sash, and units, while excluding on-site fabrication.
sources.title: NAICS Code Description: 321911 Wood Window and Door Manufacturing

sources.supports: Shows the largest occupations in other wood product manufacturing are assemblers, woodworking and sawing machine operators, material movers, truck operators, supervisors, carpenters, drivers, sales representatives, and managers.
sources.title: Data tables for OEWS industry charts, May 2024

sources.supports: Documents loading, machine operation, placement, inspection, monitoring, packing, recording, and material-movement tasks in production work.
sources.title: O*NET: Helpers--Production Workers

sources.supports: Describes AI camera inspection of frames, sash, doors, glazing, seals, finishes, and hardware across cutting, welding, glazing, and packing stations.
sources.title: AI Defect Detection for Windows and Doors

sources.supports: Reports 3.8% current US business AI use and 6.5% planned use within six months in late 2023, indicating a low starting base.
sources.title: Only 3.8% of Businesses Use AI to Produce Goods and Services

sources.supports: Reports a total recordable injury and illness rate of 3.3 cases per 100 full-time workers for NAICS 321911 in 2024.
sources.title: BLS Table 1: Incidence rates of nonfatal occupational injuries and illnesses by industry, 2024

sources.supports: States door demand is driven by residential construction and economic conditions; identifies service, quality, innovation, price, and customization as competitive methods; and describes near-term construction and remodeling weakness.
sources.title: Owens Corning 2025 Form 10-K

sources.supports: Reports 14% of working small-business owners planned a sale, IPO, or transfer within five years, rising to 17% among owners age 55 or older.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Reports May 2026 housing starts at a 1.177 million annual rate, 8.7% below May 2025, and permits at 1.413 million.
sources.title: Monthly New Residential Construction, May 2026

sources.supports: Projects owner-occupied improvement and maintenance spending growth to slow sharply in early 2027 and explains that the measure is nominal and has a four-quarter horizon.
sources.title: Leading Indicator of Remodeling Activity

title: Wood Window and Door Manufacturing
